title: Full review
rationale: No specialist review produced a blocking concern.

all_reports.summary: The checkpointflow codebase demonstrates a well-structured, layered architecture with clean unidirectional dependency flow (models <- persistence <- engine <- cli/gui), no circular imports, and a disciplined step-dispatch pattern that makes adding new step kinds straightforward. For a Phase 1 greenfield project, the architectural foundations are solid -- Pydantic discriminated unions for step types, a uniform Envelope response contract, and consistent base_dir threading for state isolation are all excellent design choices. Several medium-severity findings around the untyped Store interface, duplicated execution logic in workflow_ref_step, and tightly-coupled flow-control post-dispatch represent natural Phase 2 improvements rather than blocking issues.

findings.title: Persistence store returns untyped dicts instead of domain objects
findings.description: Store.get_run() returns dict[str, Any] (from sqlite3.Row), which forces callers throughout runner.py, queries.py, lifecycle.py, and gui/api.py to access run data via string keys like run['status'], run['workflow_path'], run['expected_event_schema']. This creates implicit coupling between the SQL column layout and every consumer, makes key typos invisible to the type checker, and means any schema migration requires auditing every call site across four modules. The persistence layer does not fully encapsulate its storage format.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a RunRecord TypedDict (or dataclass) in the models layer that Store maps rows into before returning. This gives type-safe attribute access, makes the contract explicit, and decouples consumers from the SQL column names. A TypedDict is the minimal change; a proper dataclass with computed properties is the ideal.

findings.title: Step execution loop duplicated between runner._execute_steps and workflow_ref_step.execute
findings.description: The core step-execution loop (iterate steps, check if-conditions, dispatch, handle SwitchStep jumps, handle EndStep termination) is implemented twice: once in runner.py _execute_steps() and again in workflow_ref_step.py execute(). The workflow_ref_step version is a simplified copy that lacks if-condition evaluation, persistence of step results, and output-schema validation. This means bug fixes or new capabilities added to the main loop (such as a new flow-control step kind) may silently fail to propagate to sub-workflow execution.
findings.recommendation: Extract a shared step-execution function that both the top-level runner and sub-workflow step can call, parameterized by whether to persist results (optional Store). This would also allow foreach_step body execution to reuse the same loop.

findings.title: Runner has hard-coded post-dispatch knowledge of AwaitEventStep and SwitchStep
findings.description: After dispatch_step returns in _execute_steps, the runner checks isinstance(step, AwaitEventStep) to halt-and-wait and isinstance(step, SwitchStep) to jump to a target step. This means the runner has intimate knowledge of specific step kinds, violating the Open/Closed principle. Adding a new flow-control step kind (e.g., retry, goto, or a conditional loop) requires modifying the runner loop rather than just adding a new step handler.
findings.recommendation: Encode flow-control directives in StepResult itself (e.g., a 'directive' field with values like 'halt_waiting', 'jump_to:<step_id>', 'terminate'). The runner loop would then react to generic directives rather than type-checking step classes. Acceptable for Phase 1 since the set of flow-control steps is small and stable.

findings.title: Parallel branches share mutable RunContext.step_outputs without isolation
findings.description: In parallel_step.py, all branches receive the same RunContext with a shared step_outputs dict reference. Although current branch handlers only read from step_outputs, any future branch that writes to it (e.g., a sub-workflow composing outputs back) would produce a data race under ThreadPoolExecutor. The shared mutable state is an accident waiting to happen.
findings.recommendation: Pass each branch a shallow copy of step_outputs (dict.copy()) to ensure isolation. Merged outputs are already collected safely via the futures pattern. This is a trivial, defensive change.

findings.title: Store.update_run uses **kwargs with runtime column validation instead of explicit parameters
findings.description: update_run(run_id, **kwargs) validates column names at runtime against a frozenset whitelist (_UPDATABLE_COLUMNS). Callers can pass any keyword and typos only surface as PersistenceError at runtime, not as type errors during static analysis. Combined with the dict return type, the entire persistence contract is stringly-typed.
findings.recommendation: For Phase 2, switch to explicit keyword-only parameters (status: str | None = None, current_step_id: str | None = None, etc.) or a dedicated UpdateFields dataclass. This makes the API self-documenting and type-checkable under mypy strict.

findings.title: query_status re-reads workflow YAML to reconstruct wait details
findings.description: query_status() in queries.py loads and parses the workflow YAML file to extract prompt, audience, summary, and risk_level for waiting runs, because these fields are not persisted in the runs table. This creates a runtime dependency on the workflow file still existing and being unchanged, and silently degrades to defaults if the file is missing.
findings.recommendation: Persist the WaitDetail fields (or a serialized WaitDetail JSON) in the runs table when transitioning to 'waiting' status, so query_status can reconstruct the full envelope purely from persisted state.

findings.title: Step handler signature inconsistency for parallel_step
findings.description: Seven of eight step handlers follow the signature execute(step, ctx) -> StepResult. ParallelStep requires an extra keyword argument 'workflow_steps', forcing dispatch_step to special-case it. This prevents defining a uniform StepHandler protocol and makes the dispatch less uniform.
findings.recommendation: Add workflow_steps to RunContext (or a broader ExecutionContext wrapping RunContext) so all handlers access it uniformly. This also benefits foreach_step.py which currently receives workflow_steps through its body-step dispatch calls.

findings.title: PersistenceError silently suppressed during step execution status update
findings.description: In _execute_steps(), the status update to 'running' uses contextlib.suppress(PersistenceError). If the database is corrupt or the run was externally deleted mid-execution, the error is swallowed and the step proceeds, potentially writing step results to a non-existent run.
findings.recommendation: At minimum, log or emit a warning when the status update fails. Consider failing fast if the underlying error indicates the run no longer exists, to avoid orphaned step results.

all_reports.summary: The codebase demonstrates strong security practices across all major attack surfaces. YAML parsing uses safe_load everywhere, SQL queries are fully parameterized with a column-name whitelist on dynamic updates, the web GUI has bearer token authentication and security headers, and the expression evaluator is a restricted DSL with no eval/exec. Nine non-blocking findings remain, primarily around defense-in-depth hardening for shell command interpolation, SSRF in the API step, and CSP tightening.

findings.title: Shell command interpolation from workflow inputs with no sanitization
findings.description: In cli_step.py line 58, user-supplied workflow inputs are interpolated directly into shell commands via the evaluator's interpolate() function. The resolved command is then passed to subprocess.run with shell=True (in the default/cmd case) or as an argument to bash -c. A workflow command like 'echo ${inputs.name}' will have the input value substituted verbatim. If a workflow author uses unsanitized external input in a command template and an attacker controls that input, shell metacharacter injection is possible. This is partially by-design for a workflow engine, but there is no mechanism to warn authors or escape interpolated values.
findings.recommendation: Document the trust model prominently: workflow authors are responsible for input sanitization when interpolating into shell commands. Consider offering a shlex.quote() wrapper function accessible in expressions, or an optional 'sanitize: true' flag on CLI steps that auto-escapes interpolated values.

findings.title: API step allows requests to arbitrary URLs including internal network
findings.description: The api_step.py validates that the URL scheme is http or https (line 31) but does not restrict requests to internal/private IP ranges (127.0.0.1, 10.x.x.x, 169.254.169.254, etc.). A workflow that interpolates user input into the URL field could be used for SSRF, probing internal services or accessing cloud metadata endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Consider blocking well-known metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254) by default. Add an optional deny-list for private IP ranges. At minimum, document the SSRF risk for workflow authors who interpolate user input into API step URLs.

findings.title: Auth token printed to console and exposed in browser URL bar
findings.description: In server.py lines 203-204, the full auth token is printed to stdout and passed as a query parameter when opening the browser. The token appears in terminal scrollback, browser address bar, browser history, and may be logged by HTTP proxies. The token is also accepted via query parameter (line 86), which is less secure than header-only auth.
findings.recommendation: Consider a token-exchange flow: the initial URL contains a one-time code that sets an HttpOnly session cookie, then subsequent API calls use the cookie. This keeps the long-lived token out of URL history and proxy logs.

findings.title: No CORS configuration on the web API
findings.description: The Starlette application does not configure CORS middleware. While the server binds to localhost only and requires a bearer token, a malicious webpage could attempt cross-origin requests to localhost:8420. The token requirement mitigates exploitation, but adding explicit CORS restrictions provides defense-in-depth. Simple requests (GET without custom headers) bypass preflight, though they would still need the token.
findings.recommendation: Add CORSMiddleware with allow_origins restricted to http://localhost:8420 (or the actual origin). This ensures browsers block cross-origin requests at the preflight stage.

findings.title: Content-Security-Policy allows unsafe-inline for scripts
findings.description: The CSP header includes 'unsafe-inline' in script-src (line 35). This weakens XSS protection -- any injected inline script would execute. While the GUI serves pre-built static assets and API responses are JSON, this should be tightened if possible.
findings.recommendation: If the frontend build outputs only external JS files, remove 'unsafe-inline' from script-src. If inline scripts are required by the build tooling, use nonce-based CSP with a per-request nonce.

findings.title: workflow_ref step accepts arbitrary file paths without restriction
findings.description: WorkflowRefStep.workflow_ref becomes Path(step.workflow_ref) with no path traversal checks (workflow_ref_step.py line 23). A workflow could reference files anywhere on the filesystem. While this would fail YAML parsing for non-workflow files, it could leak file existence information. The CLI tool runs with user privileges so there is no privilege escalation, but it diverges from the path traversal guards used elsewhere (loader.py, GUI API).
findings.recommendation: Apply path traversal checks consistent with loader.py (block '..' components). Consider restricting workflow_ref to paths relative to the parent workflow directory.

findings.title: No depth limit on recursive sub-workflow execution
findings.description: workflow_ref_step.py allows sub-workflows to reference other sub-workflows with no recursion depth limit. A circular or deeply nested workflow_ref chain would cause stack overflow or resource exhaustion.
findings.recommendation: Add a maximum recursion depth counter (e.g., 10 levels) passed through RunContext. Reject execution if the limit is exceeded.

findings.title: Internal error details exposed in envelope messages
findings.description: In runner.py lines 238 and 365, Python exception messages (which may contain file paths, database errors, or stack details) are included verbatim in the envelope output via 'Internal error: {exc}'. The GUI API also returns these to HTTP clients.
findings.recommendation: Log the full exception internally while returning a generic error message in the envelope. Offer a --verbose flag for CLI debugging that includes details.

findings.title: SQLite database and run artifacts created with default permissions
findings.description: The ~/.checkpointflow directory and its contents are created with default OS permissions (store.py line 85). On multi-user Unix systems this could expose workflow data to other users. On the primary target platform (single-user Windows), default ACLs on the user home directory provide adequate protection.
findings.recommendation: On Unix, set the base directory to mode 0o700 on creation. On Windows the current behavior is acceptable.

all_reports.summary: The test suite is well-structured with 378 passing tests across 38 test files, providing strong coverage of all major subsystems (engine, models, persistence, CLI, GUI, steps). Test organization follows a clear pattern with both unit and integration tests, and security-relevant paths are well-covered. However, there are notable coverage gaps in error/edge-case paths (catch-all exception handlers, parallel branch failures, unknown status codes), test organization issues (duplicated helpers, round-based test files), and minor fragilities (time.sleep for ordering, private attribute access).

findings.title: No dedicated tests for discovery.py module
findings.description: The discovery.py module (shared workflow discovery logic) has no dedicated test file. It is exercised indirectly through test_cli_flows.py and test_gui_discover.py, but those tests use monkeypatching and mock the discovery module differently. The shared discover_workflows() function in discovery.py is not directly tested for its own contract: search_dirs=None default behavior, handling of non-existent directories, deduplication by resolved path, or error resilience when YAML files are unreadable.
findings.recommendation: Add a test_discovery.py that directly tests discover_workflows() with controlled search_dirs, covering: empty dirs, duplicate paths, unreadable files, mixed valid/invalid YAML, and the default search_dirs=None path.

findings.title: No test for run_workflow/resume_workflow internal error catch-all handlers
findings.description: The run_workflow() function (line 233) and resume_workflow() (line 367) both have catch-all 'except Exception' handlers that wrap unexpected errors into ERR_INTERNAL envelopes with exit_code 90. These handlers are critical safety nets but are never exercised by any test. If the error wrapping logic had a bug (e.g., serialization failure), it would go undetected.
findings.recommendation: Add a test that mocks an internal function (e.g., Store.__init__) to raise an unexpected exception and verify the ERR_INTERNAL envelope is returned with exit_code 90.

findings.title: Missing tests for concurrent parallel branch edge cases
findings.description: The parallel_step tests cover basic success, branch failure, and branch-not-found cases, but do not test: (1) all branches failing simultaneously, (2) exception propagation from ThreadPoolExecutor (lines 77-78 in parallel_step.py where future.result() can raise), (3) parallel execution with more than 2 branches, or (4) empty branches list.
findings.recommendation: Add tests for: all-branches-failing (verify error message aggregation), 3+ parallel branches, and a branch that raises an unexpected exception to verify the error handling in the executor loop.

findings.title: Implicit workflow completion (no end step) untested at runner level
findings.description: The _execute_steps function has a fallback path (runner.py lines 212-218) for workflows that complete without an explicit end step -- it sets status to 'completed' and returns a success envelope. While foreach and workflow_ref exercise this path indirectly, there is no direct runner-level integration test with a workflow that has only CLI steps and no end step.
findings.recommendation: Add a test: workflow with only a cli step (no end step), verify run_workflow returns ok=True with status='completed' and exit_code=0.

findings.title: No test for --version CLI flag
findings.description: The _version_callback (cli.py lines 33-36) and the --version/-v option are untested. While trivial, every other CLI command has at least a help smoke test. The version callback uses typer.Exit() with no code, which is a distinct code path from _emit().
findings.recommendation: Add: result = runner.invoke(app, ['--version']); assert result.exit_code == 0; assert __version__ in result.stdout

findings.title: Duplicate helper functions across test files
findings.description: Several test files define nearly identical helper functions: _write_await_workflow appears in test_runner_resume.py, test_cli_cancel.py, test_cli_resume.py, and test_queries.py with slight variations. _make_completed_run appears in test_delete_run.py, test_gui_server.py, and test_gui_bulk_delete.py. _run_to_waiting appears in test_runner_resume.py, test_cli_cancel.py, and test_cli_resume.py. This duplication increases maintenance burden.
findings.recommendation: Extract the most common helpers into conftest.py as shared fixtures or a tests/helpers.py module. The existing write_workflow factory fixture is a good pattern to extend.

findings.title: Test files organized by bug-fix round rather than feature area
findings.description: test_coverage_gaps.py, test_bugfixes_round2.py, test_feedback_fixes.py, and test_security_fixes.py are organized by when bugs were fixed rather than by feature area. End-step interpolation is tested in test_bugfixes_round2.py and test_feedback_fixes.py rather than test_step_end.py. This makes it harder to find all tests for a given feature and assess coverage by reading test file names.
findings.recommendation: Consider gradually migrating tests from round-based files into feature-based files. For example, move end-step interpolation tests into test_step_end.py, and shell tests into test_step_cli.py.

findings.title: time.sleep used for timestamp ordering in persistence tests
findings.description: test_list_runs_ordered_desc (test_persistence_store.py:91) uses time.sleep(0.02) to ensure distinct timestamps, and test_updated_at_changes_on_update (line 337) uses time.sleep(0.01). These are fragile on fast systems or under heavy load.
findings.recommendation: Consider mocking Store._now() to return controlled timestamps, or accept that >= comparison (already used in line 340) is sufficient without sleep.

findings.title: Platform-specific test skip hides coverage on CI
findings.description: test_foreach_body_with_nested_foreach is skipped on non-Windows with reason 'echo syntax is platform-specific'. Since the project targets Python 3.11+ and most CI runs on Linux, this test never runs in CI. The test could be rewritten with python -c to be cross-platform.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite to use a platform-agnostic command like 'python -c "print(...)"' instead of relying on echo syntax differences.

findings.title: No test for _status_to_exit_code with unknown status value
findings.description: The _status_to_exit_code function in queries.py (line 160) returns ExitCode.SUCCESS for unknown status values via the dict .get() default. This implicit behavior is not tested. If a new status like 'timeout' were added without updating this mapping, it would silently return success exit code.
findings.recommendation: Add a test asserting _status_to_exit_code('unknown') returns ExitCode.SUCCESS (documenting intentional fallback), or change the implementation to raise for unknown statuses.

findings.title: run_ctx fixture name suggests it returns a context, not a factory
findings.description: The run_ctx conftest fixture returns a Callable that creates RunContext instances. Every test that uses it must call run_ctx() with parentheses. The naming could mislead readers into thinking it returns a RunContext directly.
findings.recommendation: Consider renaming to make_run_ctx or run_ctx_factory to clarify the factory pattern.

findings.title: Tests depend on examples/ directory files existing
findings.description: Several tests (test_schema.py, test_coverage_gaps.py, test_models_workflow.py, test_cli_validate.py) reference files in examples/ like 'examples/publish-confluence-change.yaml'. These rely on files checked into the repo. If those files are renamed or removed, tests break.
findings.recommendation: This is acceptable for regression tests, but consider marking them with @pytest.mark.examples so they can be identified and optionally skipped.

all_reports.summary: The codebase is well-structured with clear module boundaries, strong type safety via mypy strict mode, and good test coverage (~6k lines of tests for ~3k lines of source). However, there are several DRY violations in step result serialization and workflow metadata propagation, elevated complexity in runner.py's core execution functions, and a silent error suppression that could mask persistence bugs. These are all fixable without architectural changes.

findings.title: Step result serialization duplicated between queries.py and gui/api.py
findings.description: The dict comprehension that converts a step_results row into a clean dict (step_id, step_kind, exit_code, error_code, error_message, outputs via json.loads, stdout_path, stderr_path, created_at) is copy-pasted nearly identically in query_inspect() (queries.py lines 122-131) and get_run_detail() (gui/api.py lines 36-46). The gui/api.py version adds execution_order. If the schema changes, both must be updated in lockstep.
findings.recommendation: Extract a shared _serialize_step_result(sr) helper function in the persistence or models layer. Both queries.py and gui/api.py should call it. The gui version can merge in execution_order after the call.

findings.title: Event serialization duplicated between queries.py and gui/api.py
findings.description: The same event dict comprehension (event_name, event_data from json.loads, created_at) appears in both query_inspect() (queries.py lines 135-141) and get_run_detail() (gui/api.py lines 50-56). Same maintenance risk as the step result duplication.
findings.recommendation: Extract a shared _serialize_event(ev) helper alongside the step result helper.

findings.title: Workflow metadata kwargs repeated across lifecycle, queries, and runner
findings.description: The pattern of extracting workflow_id, workflow_name, workflow_description, workflow_version from a run dict (with .get() for nullable fields) is repeated in lifecycle.py (lines 42-46 and 83-87), queries.py (lines 36-42 and 151-155), and runner.py (lines 82-87 and 544-547). This is 6 occurrences of the same 4-key dict extraction, creating a maintenance burden when the workflow metadata fields change.
findings.recommendation: Add a helper like _workflow_meta_from_run(run: dict) -> dict[str, Any] that extracts these four fields from a run dict. For the runner, the existing wf_meta dict built from the Workflow object already consolidates this; the run-dict variant should get the same treatment.

findings.title: _execute_steps function has elevated cyclomatic complexity
findings.description: The _execute_steps function in runner.py (lines 70-218) is ~150 lines and handles condition evaluation, step dispatch, result persistence, output merging, AwaitEventStep halting, EndStep termination, and SwitchStep jump logic. It has roughly 10+ branch points in a single while loop. While the logic is linear and readable today, the function does too many things, making future modifications risky.
findings.recommendation: Extract the per-step-type post-dispatch handling (AwaitEventStep -> waiting envelope, EndStep -> completion envelope, SwitchStep -> jump) into named helper functions. This would reduce the while-loop body to ~30 lines and make each concern testable in isolation.

findings.title: _resume_workflow_inner is long with interleaved validation and execution
findings.description: The _resume_workflow_inner function (runner.py lines 373-570) is ~200 lines mixing five sequential validation checks (each producing Envelope.failure), state restoration, transition evaluation, and step execution dispatch. The validation and execution concerns are interleaved in one function.
findings.recommendation: Consider splitting into a _validate_resume_preconditions() function that returns either an error Envelope or a validated context tuple, followed by the actual execution logic. This matches the existing pattern where run_workflow delegates to _run_workflow_inner.

findings.title: Sub-workflow execution loop in workflow_ref_step duplicates runner logic
findings.description: workflow_ref_step.execute() (lines 63-117) reimplements a mini execution loop that handles step iteration, SwitchStep jump resolution, EndStep termination, and AwaitEventStep rejection. This parallels the logic in runner._execute_steps but without persistence, without if-condition support, and without event handling. As new step behaviors are added, this secondary loop must be kept in sync manually.
findings.recommendation: Consider extracting a shared 'execute step sequence' function that both the main runner and workflow_ref_step can call, parameterized by whether to persist results. This would eliminate the duplicate control-flow logic.

findings.title: Silent suppression of PersistenceError on status update
findings.description: In _execute_steps (runner.py line 125), 'with contextlib.suppress(PersistenceError)' silently swallows errors when updating run status to 'running'. If the database is corrupted or the run was deleted mid-execution, this masks the real problem and the run continues executing against a stale or missing record.
findings.recommendation: Either log the suppressed error or handle it explicitly. If this suppress is a workaround for a known edge case, document the reason with a comment.

findings.title: update_run performs a SELECT before every UPDATE
findings.description: Store.update_run() (store.py line 169) calls self.get_run(run_id) to verify the run exists before executing the UPDATE. This doubles the database round-trips for every status update. In _execute_steps, update_run is called at least twice per step (status update + step_outputs_json), so this adds measurable overhead for workflows with many steps.
findings.recommendation: Replace the existence check with UPDATE ... WHERE run_id = ? and check cursor.rowcount. If rowcount == 0, raise PersistenceError. This halves the database calls and is also atomic.

findings.title: switch_step silently swallows EvaluatorError in case evaluation
findings.description: switch_step.execute() catches EvaluatorError and continues to the next case (line 17). This means a malformed condition expression is indistinguishable from a condition that simply didn't match. A workflow author debugging a switch that always hits the default would have no indication that one of their conditions has a syntax error.
findings.recommendation: Collect the evaluation errors and include them in the failure message if no case matches and no default is defined. This aids debugging without changing the happy-path behavior.

findings.title: Re-exports in runner.py use noqa and bottom-of-file imports
findings.description: runner.py lines 574-575 re-export cancel_run and delete_run from lifecycle.py with # noqa: E402 comments for the late import. This backward-compatibility shim means cli.py imports these through runner.py rather than directly from lifecycle.py. Since this is a greenfield project per CLAUDE.md, backward compatibility with external consumers is unlikely to be needed.
findings.recommendation: Update cli.py to import cancel_run and delete_run directly from checkpointflow.engine.lifecycle, then remove the re-exports from runner.py. This eliminates the noqa suppression and the unusual bottom-of-file import pattern.
